---
title: "Monte Carlo Simulation Design and Diagnostics"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
execute:
  echo: false
---

# Purpose

This project runs Monte Carlo simulations for high-dimensional instrumental
variable quantile regression (IVQR). The simulation design focuses on weak
instruments and high-dimensional controls, where identification, variable
selection, confidence-region inversion, and computation can all affect finite
sample performance.

# Estimators

The clean simulation pipeline compares four final estimators.

| Estimator | Role | Main limitation |
|---|---|---|
| Oracle IVQR | Benchmark using the true relevant controls. | Infeasible outside simulation because the true control set is unknown. |
| Post-selection IVQR | Feasible IVQR after selecting controls. | Sensitive to selection mistakes and omitted selection uncertainty. |
| Full-control IVQR | IVQR using all available controls. | Computationally expensive and unstable when the control dimension is large. |
| DML-IVQR | Cross-fitted residualized IVQR for high-dimensional nuisance adjustment. | Can be conservative and depends on nuisance-model quality. |

# Structural IVQR Target

The structural quantile condition is

$$
P(Y \le D \alpha_\tau + X' \beta_\tau \mid X, Z) = \tau.
$$

Here, $\alpha_\tau$ is the structural quantile treatment/effect parameter at
quantile $\tau$. The controls $X$ and instruments $Z$ enter the conditional
quantile restriction used to construct IVQR confidence regions.

# Confidence Region Construction

The confidence region is constructed by inverting the IVQR test over an alpha
grid:

$$
CR_\tau = \{a \in A : T_n(a) \le c_\tau\}.
$$

Here, $A$ is the alpha grid, $T_n(a)$ is the inverted IVQR test statistic, and
$c_\tau$ is the critical value. Boundary hits indicate that the alpha grid may
be too narrow. Disconnected regions can occur under weak or nonstandard
identification and should be reported as a diagnostic rather than hidden.

# Simulation Modes

| Mode | Replications |
|---|---:|
| fast | 10 |
| full | 500 |

# Design Grid

| Component | Values |
|---|---|
| DGPs | dgp1, dgp2, dgp3 |
| n | 500, 1000 |
| p | 200, 500 |
| pi | 1.0, 0.5, 0.25, 0.10 |
| tau | 0.25, 0.50, 0.75 |

The parameter $\pi$ controls instrument strength. Smaller values of $\pi$
correspond to weaker instruments and are expected to produce wider, less stable,
or more irregular confidence regions.

# Main Diagnostics

| Diagnostic | Meaning |
|---|---|
| coverage | whether CR contains true alpha |
| CR length | informativeness/precision |
| boundary hit | grid truncation warning |
| disconnected CR | weak/nonstandard identification diagnostic |
| failed-alpha rate | numerical reliability over alpha grid |
| RMSE | point-estimate accuracy |
| MAE | point-estimate accuracy |
| runtime | computational cost |

# Recommended Reporting

Final thesis tables should report at minimum: coverage, CR length, boundary hit
rate, disconnected CR rate, failed-alpha rate, RMSE, MAE, and runtime.

# Commands

Fast default:

```powershell
pixi run fast
```

Full default:

```powershell
pixi run full
```

Fast all estimators:

```powershell
pixi run python scenarios/run_simulation.py --mode fast --estimators oracle post_selection full_control dml --n-jobs 4 --batch-size 10 --alpha-grid-size 21 --output results/raw/fast_all.csv --manifest results/raw/fast_all_manifest.json
```

Full default with explicit output:

```powershell
pixi run python scenarios/run_simulation.py --mode full --n-jobs 4 --batch-size 10 --alpha-grid-size 21 --output results/raw/full_results.csv --manifest results/raw/full_manifest.json
```
